---
pagetitle: "About - Architecture 2.0"
description: "Architecture 2.0 develops the engineering discipline of using AI to formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend computer architecture decisions."
format:
  html:
    page-layout: custom
    toc: false
---

```{=html}
<div class="site-wrap site-narrow">
<header class="site-masthead">
  <span class="pill">Architecture 2.0</span>
  <span class="pill">About</span>
</header>

<section class="site-hero">
  <h1>About</h1>
  <p class="subtitle">A research and teaching project for AI-assisted computer architecture</p>
</section>

<section class="site-note">
  <div>
    <span class="site-note-label">Working orientation</span>
    <p>
      Architecture 2.0 is the engineering discipline of using AI, grounded in
      architectural representations, tools, and experiments, to formulate,
      explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend computer architecture
      decisions.
    </p>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="site-section">
  <div class="section-heading">
    <h2>The idea</h2>
  </div>
  <p class="site-lede">
  Computer architects formulate problems, explore alternatives, implement
  mechanisms, evaluate them with tools and experiments, explain why results
  changed, and defend decisions under constraints. AI can now assist across that
  work, extending what a team can consider and evaluate. Architecture 2.0 asks
  how to use that assistance without detaching a result from the architectural
  representations, mechanisms, evidence, and judgment that make it credible.
  </p>
  <p class="site-lede">
  Design loops remain essential execution and analysis mechanisms, but they do
  not define the field. Much of the effort here makes those loops explicit and
  auditable by defining the
  <strong>state</strong> an agent reasons over, the <strong>actions</strong> it may
  take, the <strong>rejection</strong> criteria that disqualify a bad result, and
  the <strong>evidence</strong> that makes a claim believable. The
  <a href="/book/index.html">synthesis lecture</a> develops that vocabulary; the
  <a href="/tools/index.html">tool registry</a> collects the simulators, models, and
  harnesses that make it runnable.
  </p>
</section>

<section class="site-section">
  <div class="section-heading">
    <h2>Shared infrastructure</h2>
    <p>Architecture 2.0 is organized around the shared infrastructure required for credible AI-assisted architecture.</p>
  </div>
  <p class="site-lede">
  Turning machine learning loose on architecture needs shared datasets,
  comparable benchmarks, inspectable tools, and reproducible evidence. It also
  needs people trained to work across both fields. Architecture 2.0 grew out of
  research at Harvard and exposes a set of artifacts that other groups can
  inspect, test, and extend. The work organizes into a few strands:
  </p>
  <div class="site-strands cols-2">
    <div class="strand">
      <span class="site-pill">Data</span>
      <h3>Datasets &amp; benchmarks</h3>
      <p>Shared, versioned data and comparable benchmarks so results mean the same
      thing across groups.</p>
    </div>
    <div class="strand">
      <span class="site-pill">Methods</span>
      <h3>Methods</h3>
      <p>The learning, search, and agentic methods that generate, predict, and
      optimize designs inside bounded architecture studies.</p>
    </div>
    <div class="strand">
      <span class="site-pill">Tools</span>
      <h3>Tools &amp; infrastructure</h3>
      <p>Simulators, proxy models, and verification harnesses that connect
      methods to architectural evidence.</p>
    </div>
    <div class="strand">
      <span class="site-pill">Evidence</span>
      <h3>Reproducibility &amp; evidence</h3>
      <p>Practices that let reviewers replay declared parts of a study and check
      the evidence behind an architectural claim.</p>
    </div>
    <div class="strand">
      <span class="site-pill">Teaching</span>
      <h3>Education &amp; workforce</h3>
      <p>Courses and materials for a generation of architects fluent in both
      systems and machine learning.</p>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="site-section">
  <div class="section-heading">
    <h2>Where it started</h2>
  </div>
  <p class="site-lede">
  The community behind this work has been meeting for years. Since 2020, the
  <a href="https://sites.google.com/view/mlarchsys">MLArchSys</a> workshop at ISCA
  (the International Symposium on Computer Architecture) has brought the machine
  learning, systems, and architecture communities together around learning for
  hardware and hardware for learning, including meetings in Tokyo (2025) and
  Raleigh (2026).
  </p>
  <p class="site-lede">
  As foundation models and autonomous agents began to reshape how systems are
  designed, that community turned toward agentic approaches. MLArchSys 2026 added a
  dedicated <strong>A³ (Agentic Approaches to Architecture)</strong> track, and the
  <a href="https://harvard-edge.github.io/isca-26-arch-2-workshop/">Architecture 2.0
  workshop at ISCA 2026</a> focused on agentic AI for computing-systems design,
  anchored in computer architecture and hardware/software co-design. An earlier
  gathering, opened by a keynote from <strong>Partha Ranganathan</strong> (Google),
  helped map the datasets, tools, and training the field would need.
  </p>
  <p class="site-lede">
  Architecture 2.0 itself grew out of Harvard's
  <a href="https://harvard-edge.github.io/cs249r_fall2025/">CS249r</a> graduate
  seminar (Fall 2025) and the synthesis lecture developed alongside it. It is part
  of the <a href="https://mlsysbook.ai">mlsysbook.ai</a> family of open,
  community-built learning resources.
  </p>
</section>

<section class="site-section">
  <div class="section-heading">
    <h2>Take part</h2>
    <p>Use the hub as the front door for reading, contributing, and teaching.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="site-strands cols-2">
    <div class="strand">
      <span class="site-pill">Contribute</span>
      <h3>Add a tool</h3>
      <p>Built an open simulator, surrogate model, or agentic loop?
      <a href="https://github.com/harvard-edge/arch2/issues/new?template=submit_tool.yml">Propose
      it for the registry</a>.</p>
    </div>
    <div class="strand">
      <span class="site-pill">Read</span>
      <h3>Read &amp; cite</h3>
      <p>Use the <a href="/book/index.html">synthesis lecture</a> in your own work,
      and tell us where it is wrong.</p>
    </div>
    <div class="strand">
      <span class="site-pill">Discuss</span>
      <h3>Discuss and review</h3>
      <p>Use
      <a href="https://github.com/harvard-edge/arch2/discussions">Discussions</a>
      for questions or proposals that should remain searchable, and contribute
      reviewed changes on <a href="https://github.com/harvard-edge/arch2">GitHub</a>.</p>
    </div>
    <div class="strand">
      <span class="site-pill">Teach</span>
      <h3>Teach with it</h3>
      <p>Adapt the book and course materials for your own students and reading
      groups.</p>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

</div>
```
